results.title: Topolines: Generate Topologic contours
results.content:
Promoted

Alexis Oulès

📌

Hey Product Hunt! 👋 I built Topolines to solve a recurring friction in my own design workflow: generating clean, vector-based topographic contour maps without opening heavy 3D software or manual vector tools. With Topolines, you can dynamically tweak elevation density, noise, line weights, and colors, then export clean SVG or HD PNG files ready for Figma, web projects, or print. Give it a try and let me know what you think! I'm around all day to answer your questions and take feature requests. 🙌 [...] Subscribe

results.title: How to Read a Topo Map
results.content:
Contour lines indicate the steepness of terrain. Contour lines connect points that share the same elevation: Where they're close together (they never intersect), elevation is changing rapidly in short distance and the terrain is steep. Where contour lines are wide apart, elevation is changing slowly, indicating a gentle slope.

topographic contour lines translated to steep slopes and gentle slopes

Contour lines also indicate the shape of the terrain. Roughly concentric circles are probably showing you a peak, and areas between peaks are passes. Studying a topo map of a familiar area is a great way to learn how to match terrain features with the contour lines on a map.

2 D topographic contours translated as 3 D [...] The legend also lists key data like the map's scale, contour- and index-line intervals, grid systems (used for more advanced navigation) and magnetic declination (needed to set up your compass).

topo map examples

Practice reading features from a map of a familiar area. Visualize how the terrain on the major landmarks relates to the contour lines on your map. Pick out features like peaks and saddles. Identify subtler features like cliffs, which have contour lines grouped tightly together, and ridgelines, which connect peaks and have contour lines that decrease in elevation on each side. Valleys are low-elevation areas between ridgelines; some might have a creek running along the bottom, though that isn't a requirement for a feature to be a valley. [...] 2 D topographic contours translated as 3 D

Index contour lines: Every fifth contour line is a thicker, "index" line. At some point along that line, its exact elevation is listed.

Contour interval: The change in elevation from one contour line to the next is always the same within the same map. Many maps have either a 40- or 80-foot contour interval: An 80-foot interval simply means that each contour line is 80 vertical feet away from the next closest line. You find the contour interval for your map in its legend.

Every once in a while, a circle indicates a depression rather than a peak. A circle with tick marks inside it indicates a depression, rather than a peak. You should also see elevations decreasing as you get near the depression.

## Map Scales
